title: Classic Anapanasati (Breath Meditation)
text: Find a comfortable seated position. Sit upright with your spine straight but not rigid. Close your eyes gently. Begin by taking three deep breaths to settle the body. Now let the breath return to its natural rhythm. Do not try to control the breath in any way. Simply observe the natural flow of air in and out. Bring your attention to the tip of your nostrils, the small area just below the nose where you can feel the air entering and leaving. This is your anchor point. Keep your attention fixed here. Feel the sensation of the air as it enters -- it may be cool. Feel the sensation as it leaves -- it may be warm. That is all. Just this sensation, at this spot. When the mind wanders -- and it will wander -- simply notice that it has wandered. Do not judge yourself, do not follow the thought. Gently, patiently, bring your attention back to the breath at the nostrils. Each time you notice the mind has wandered and bring it back, you are strengthening your concentration. This is the practice. You will do this many, many times. The breath at the nostrils. When the breath is long, know that the breath is long. When the breath is short, know that the breath is short. Do not try to make the breath longer or shorter. Simply know the breath as it is. Continue this for the duration of the sitting. When thoughts arise, note them as 'thinking' and return to the breath. When sounds arise, note them as 'hearing' and return to the breath. When sensations arise in the body, note them as 'feeling' and return to the breath. Always return to the breath at the nostrils.

title: Goenka-style Vipassana Body Scan
text: Sit with your spine erect, your hands resting on your knees or in your lap. Close your eyes. Begin with awareness of the breath at the nostrils for a few minutes to sharpen your attention. Now move your attention to the top of your head. Feel any sensation there -- tingling, pressure, warmth, coolness, pulsing, or perhaps no clear sensation at all. Whatever is there, simply observe it. Do not react. Do not wish it were different. Now move your attention slowly downward. Feel the sensations on your forehead. Your temples. The area around your eyes. Your cheeks. Your nose. Your mouth and lips. Your jaw. Your chin. Move slowly, systematically. Feel each area. Detect the boundary between sensation and no-sensation. Now your neck. The front of your neck, the sides, the back. Your throat. Move to your shoulders. Feel the weight there, any tension, any tingling. Your upper arms. Your elbows. Your forearms. Your wrists. Your hands. Each finger, one by one if you can. Feel the subtle sensations -- tingling, vibration, warmth, coolness, pressure. Now your chest. The rise and fall of breathing. Your ribcage expanding and contracting. Your upper back. Your middle back. Your lower back. Your abdomen. Feel the movement of the abdomen with each breath. Your hips. Your upper thighs. Your knees. Your calves. Your ankles. Your feet. The soles of your feet. Your toes. Now sweep your attention back upward through the entire body in one continuous flow. From the tips of your toes to the top of your head. Feel the entire body as a field of sensation. Then sweep downward again. Continue this sweeping. The key is equanimity -- observe every sensation, pleasant or unpleasant, with perfect balance of mind. Do not crave pleasant sensations. Do not have aversion toward unpleasant sensations. Simply observe. Anicca -- impermanence. Every sensation arises and passes away.

title: Transcendental Meditation Style Mantra Practice
text: Sit comfortably with your eyes closed. Allow your body to settle. There is nothing to do, nothing to force, nothing to concentrate on. Simply sit and rest. After a moment of rest, gently introduce the mantra. The mantra is a sound without meaning. Begin to think the mantra effortlessly, as if it were a faint thought arising on its own. Do not try to pronounce it clearly in your mind. Do not try to keep rhythm. Let the mantra come easily, faintly, almost as a whisper in your mind. If the mantra becomes unclear or fades away, that is fine. Let it go. When you notice you are thinking other thoughts, gently return to the mantra. No effort. No concentration. The mantra is not something you hold onto tightly -- it is something you allow to happen. If you fall asleep, that is fine. If you drift into silence, that is fine. If you have many thoughts, that is fine. Whatever happens is correct. The only instruction is: when you realize the mantra is no longer present, easily start it again. Twenty minutes. Then stop repeating the mantra and sit with eyes closed for two or three minutes before opening your eyes.

title: Zen Shikantaza (Just Sitting)
text: Take the posture. Sit on the cushion in full or half lotus, or seiza. Spine straight, chin slightly tucked, eyes half-open, gaze resting on the floor about three feet ahead. Hands in cosmic mudra -- left hand on top of right, thumbs lightly touching, forming an oval. This is the posture of awakening. Now: just sit. Do not follow thoughts. Do not push thoughts away. Do not concentrate on the breath. Do not concentrate on anything. Simply be present, with bright, clear awareness. Thoughts will arise. Let them arise. Let them pass. Do not pursue them. Do not engage them. Do not fight them. They are clouds passing through empty sky. The sky does not follow the clouds. You are the sky. Sounds will come. Let them come and go. Sensations will arise in the body. Let them be. There is nothing to attain, nothing to figure out, nothing to achieve. Just this moment. Just sitting. If you find yourself lost in a train of thought, notice this. You have already returned to awareness simply by noticing. No need to scold yourself. Just sit. The practice is nothing special. And yet it is the most difficult thing in the world: to simply be here, without agenda, without goal, without commentary.

title: Metta (Loving-Kindness) Full Practice
text: Sit comfortably. Close your eyes. Take a few deep breaths to settle. Now bring to mind yourself. Picture yourself sitting here. And silently repeat these phrases: May I be happy. May I be healthy. May I be safe from harm. May I live with ease. Say these phrases slowly, with intention. Feel the meaning of each wish. May I be happy. May I be healthy. May I be safe. May I live with ease. Let the feeling of kindness toward yourself grow in your heart. Now bring to mind someone you love dearly -- a close friend, family member, or partner. See their face clearly. Direct the same wishes to them: May you be happy. May you be healthy. May you be safe from harm. May you live with ease. Feel love radiating from your heart center toward this person. Now think of a neutral person -- someone you see regularly but have no strong feelings about. The postal worker, a neighbor, someone at the grocery store. Direct the wishes to them: May you be happy. May you be healthy. May you be safe. May you live with ease. Notice any resistance. Simply continue. Now bring to mind a difficult person -- someone with whom you have conflict or irritation. This is harder. Direct the wishes to them: May you be happy. May you be healthy. May you be safe from harm. May you live with ease. You are not condoning their behavior. You are wishing them well as a fellow being. Finally, extend the wishes outward to all beings everywhere. In all directions, without limit. May all beings be happy. May all beings be healthy. May all beings be safe from harm. May all beings live with ease. Feel compassion and love radiating outward from your heart to the entire world.

title: Tonglen (Giving and Taking)
text: Sit quietly. Close your eyes. Settle your body and mind with a few breaths. Now bring to mind someone who is suffering. It may be a friend who is ill, a family member in pain, or someone you have heard about. See them clearly. Feel their pain, their difficulty, their sadness. Now coordinate with your breath. As you breathe in, imagine taking on their suffering. Breathe in their pain as a dark, heavy smoke. Take it into yourself willingly. Do not be afraid. You are strong enough. As you breathe out, send them love, healing, light, relief. Breathe out bright, white, warm light that reaches them and dissolves their suffering. Breathe in the darkness of their pain. Breathe out the light of healing. Continue this rhythm. In-breath: their suffering enters you, dark and heavy. Out-breath: your love and healing reaches them, bright and warm. You may feel discomfort. That is natural. Stay with the practice. Gradually extend the practice. Take on the suffering of all beings who suffer in similar ways. Breathe in the pain of everyone who is ill. Breathe out healing for everyone who is ill. Breathe in the grief of everyone who has lost a loved one. Breathe out comfort for everyone who grieves. Let your compassion expand without limit.

title: MBSR Body Scan (Jon Kabat-Zinn style)
text: Lie down on your back in a comfortable position. You can use a mat, a blanket, a bed. Let your arms rest at your sides, palms facing up. Let your feet fall open. Close your eyes. Take a few moments to feel the weight of your body against the surface beneath you. Notice where your body makes contact with the floor -- the back of your head, your shoulder blades, your upper back, your lower back, your buttocks, the backs of your legs, your heels. Feel the pull of gravity. Now bring your attention to your left foot. Your toes. Can you feel each toe individually? The sole of your left foot. The top of your left foot. The ankle. Just feel whatever is there. Not trying to relax. Not trying to feel anything in particular. Just noticing what is already there. Whatever it is, it is okay. If you feel nothing, notice that too. Now breathe into your left foot -- imagine the breath traveling all the way down to your toes. And as you breathe out, let go of your left foot. Let it dissolve from your awareness. Move to your left lower leg. The calf. The shin. The knee. Feel whatever is there. Breathing into this area. Breathing out, letting go. The left thigh. The hip. Now move to the right foot. The right toes. Right sole. Right ankle. Right lower leg. Right knee. Right thigh. Right hip. The pelvis. The lower back. The abdomen. The chest. The upper back. The shoulders. Now both arms simultaneously. The upper arms. The elbows. The forearms. The wrists. The hands. Each finger. The neck. The face. The jaw. The cheeks. The eyes. The forehead. The top of the head. Now feel the entire body breathing. One complete whole. Alive. Present.

title: Yoga Nidra (Simplified)
text: Lie down in shavasana -- on your back, arms at your sides, palms facing up, feet falling open. Close your eyes. You will not move for the duration of this practice. Make your resolve -- your sankalpa. A short, positive statement about something you wish to manifest. Repeat it three times mentally with full conviction. Now bring your attention to your body. Become aware of your right hand thumb. Index finger. Middle finger. Ring finger. Little finger. Palm of the right hand. Back of the right hand. Right wrist. Right forearm. Right elbow. Right upper arm. Right shoulder. Right side of the chest. Right side of the abdomen. Right hip. Right thigh. Right knee. Right calf. Right ankle. Right foot. Right toes. Now the left hand thumb. Left index finger. Left middle finger. Left ring finger. Left little finger. Left palm. Left wrist. Left forearm. Left elbow. Left upper arm. Left shoulder. Left side of the chest. Left side of the abdomen. Left hip. Left thigh. Left knee. Left calf. Left ankle. Left foot. Left toes. Now the back of the body. Right shoulder blade. Left shoulder blade. The whole back. The spine. The buttocks. Now the front of the body. The throat. The chest. The navel. The abdomen. The whole front of the body. The whole body. The whole body. The whole body. Feel the heaviness of the body. The body is so heavy it is sinking into the floor. Heavy. So heavy. Now feel lightness. The body is so light it could float upward. Light. Weightless. Now return to the breath. Feel the natural breath. Count the breaths backwards from 27. If you lose count, start again from 27. Now repeat your sankalpa three times. Slowly become aware of the room. Wiggle your fingers and toes. Take a deep breath. Open your eyes when ready.

title: Dzogchen Pointing-Out Instructions (simplified)
text: Sit in a comfortable posture. Let the mind settle naturally. Do not follow the past, do not anticipate the future, do not alter the present. Let the mind rest in its natural state. Look at the mind itself. What is this awareness? Does it have a color? A shape? A location? Look directly at the one who is looking. What do you find? If you find nothing -- that is it. That open, clear, empty knowing IS the nature of mind. It is not blank emptiness. It is vivid, aware, cognizant space. Rest in that. Do not meditate. Do not not-meditate. Do not try to maintain this recognition. Do not try to let it go. Whatever arises -- thought, emotion, perception -- look at its nature. Where does it come from? Where does it go? What is it made of? If you look carefully, you find that thoughts arise from this awareness, exist as this awareness, and dissolve back into this awareness. Like waves in the ocean. The waves are not separate from the ocean. Thoughts are not separate from awareness. Rest in that recognition. This is rigpa -- the natural state of pure awareness. You do not need to create it. You do not need to find it. You only need to stop overlooking it. Simply remain in this natural wakefulness. When you get distracted, notice the distraction. The noticing IS the return. No effort needed. Just this bright, open, natural awareness.

title: Progressive Muscle Relaxation
text: Lie down or sit comfortably. Close your eyes. Take a few deep breaths. We will systematically tense and relax each muscle group in your body. Start with your feet. Curl your toes tightly. Hold the tension for five seconds. Feel the tightness, the strain. Now release. Let the tension flow out completely. Notice the contrast between tension and relaxation. Feel the warmth and heaviness as the muscles relax. Now your calves. Tighten your calf muscles by pointing your toes. Hold for five seconds. Feel the tension. Now release. Let the muscles go completely soft. Your thighs. Tighten by pressing your knees together. Hold. Feel the tension building. Now release. Feel the relaxation spreading through your legs. Your buttocks. Clench tightly. Hold. Release. Your abdomen. Tighten your stomach muscles as if bracing for a punch. Hold. Release. Feel the warmth. Your chest. Take a deep breath and hold it, tightening the chest muscles. Now breathe out and release all tension. Your hands. Make tight fists. Hold. Feel the tension in your fingers, your palms, your wrists. Release. Let your hands go completely limp. Your arms. Flex your biceps. Hold. Release. Your shoulders. Raise them up toward your ears. Hold that tension. Now drop them. Let them fall heavily. Your neck. Gently press your head back. Hold. Release. Your face. Scrunch up your face -- squeeze your eyes, clench your jaw, wrinkle your nose. Hold. Now release everything. Let your face go completely smooth and relaxed. Now scan your body from feet to head. Notice the overall sense of relaxation. Your body is heavy, warm, relaxed. Simply rest here.

title: Open Monitoring Vipassana (Mahasi style)
text: Sit comfortably with your back straight. Close your eyes. Begin with the breath. Notice the rising and falling of the abdomen. As the abdomen rises, note mentally: rising. As it falls, note: falling. Rising. Falling. Rising. Falling. Keep your attention closely on the movement. When a thought arises, note: thinking. Then return to rising, falling. When a sound is heard, note: hearing. Return to rising, falling. When an itch arises, note: itching. If it persists, you may scratch, noting: intending, moving, scratching, relief. Return to rising, falling. When an emotion arises, note: feeling. Note whether it is pleasant, unpleasant, or neutral. Return to rising, falling. When pain arises, note: pain. Observe the pain carefully. Does it throb? Does it burn? Does it move? Note: throbbing, burning, moving. If the pain becomes too intense, you may shift position, noting: intending, moving, sitting. Return to rising, falling. The key is continuous noting. Every moment of experience is noted. Nothing is ignored. Nothing is favored. Rising. Falling. Thinking. Hearing. Itching. Feeling. Pain. These are not distractions -- they are the objects of meditation. The entire field of experience is the meditation. But the primary object remains the rising and falling of the abdomen. Always return there. This develops a continuous stream of mindfulness -- moment-to-moment awareness of whatever is happening.

title: Chakra Meditation with Visualization
text: Sit comfortably with your spine straight. Close your eyes. Take three deep breaths. Now bring your attention to the base of your spine, the root chakra. Visualize a glowing red ball of energy here. Feel it pulsing with warmth. This is your connection to the earth, to safety, to stability. Breathe into this red light. Let it grow brighter with each breath. Now move your attention upward to just below your navel, the sacral chakra. Visualize a glowing orange light. This is the center of creativity and pleasure. Breathe into the orange light. Let it expand. Move upward to the solar plexus, just above the navel. Visualize a bright yellow light. This is your center of personal power and confidence. Breathe into the yellow light. Feel your inner strength. Now the heart center, in the middle of your chest. Visualize a beautiful green light. This is the center of love and compassion. Breathe into the green light. Feel your heart opening. Move to the throat. Visualize a blue light. This is the center of expression and truth. Breathe into the blue light. Feel clarity in your communication. Now the third eye, between your eyebrows. Visualize an indigo light. This is the center of intuition and insight. Breathe into the indigo light. Feel your inner vision sharpening. Finally, the crown of your head. Visualize a violet or white light. This is your connection to the infinite, to pure consciousness. Breathe into this light. Feel it expanding upward without limit. Now see all seven lights shining simultaneously -- red, orange, yellow, green, blue, indigo, violet -- a rainbow column of light along your spine. Feel the energy flowing freely through all centers. Rest here.
